original_abstract: In this work, we have fabricated an aryl amino-substituted graphitic carbon nitride (g-C<sub>3</sub>N<sub>4</sub>) catalyst with atomically dispersed Mn capable of generating hydrogen peroxide (H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>) directly from seawater. This new catalyst exhibited excellent reactivity, obtaining up to 2230 μM H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> in 7 h from alkaline water and up to 1800 μM from seawater under identical conditions. More importantly, the catalyst was quickly recovered for subsequent reuse without appreciable loss in performance. Interestingly, unlike the usual two-electron oxygen reduction reaction pathway, the generation of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> was through a less common two-electron water oxidation reaction (WOR) process in which both the direct and indirect WOR processes occurred; namely, photoinduced h<sup>+</sup> directly oxidized H<sub>2</sub>O to H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> via a one-step 2e<sup>-</sup> WOR, and photoinduced h<sup>+</sup> first oxidized a hydroxide (OH<sup>-</sup>) ion to generate a hydroxy radical (<sup>•</sup>OH), and H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> was formed indirectly by the combination of two <sup>•</sup>OH. We have characterized the material, at the catalytic sites, at the atomic level using electron paramagnetic resonance, X-ray absorption near edge structure, extended X-ray absorption fine structure, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, magic-angle spinning solid-state NMR spectroscopy, and multiscale molecular modeling, combining classical reactive molecular dynamics simulations and quantum chemistry calculations.
humanized_abstract: Herein, we report on the fabrication of an atomically dispersed Mn on an aryl amino-substituted graphitic carbon nitride (g-C<sub>3</sub>N<sub>4</sub>) catalyst (denoted as Mn/C3N4-SSC or catalytic system derived from seawater). Using only seawater as a reactant source (including solution constituents and ambient dissolved gases), the synthesized catalyst catalyzed the effective production of hydrogen peroxide (H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>). Of interest to catalytic applications, the as-prepared catalyst powder showed outstanding catalytic activity of up to 2230 μM H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> after reacting alkaline water for only 7 h and achieved a corresponding H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> concentration of 1800 μM within 7 hours by catalyzing seawater under the same experimental conditions while retaining activity after quick catalyst wash-and-use cycling. Notably, the catalytic generation of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> in seawater occurred via a rare two-electron water oxidation reaction (WOR) pathway at pH 10.8 and 8.3, where both direct and indirect processes (photoexcited holes (h<sup>+</sup>)) were found in which one involves one-step photoassisted 2e<sup>-</sup> WOR via which a photoexcited hole (h<sup>+</sup>) directly catalyzes water (H<sub>2</sub>O) oxidation to form hydrogen peroxide (H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>) and the other is sequential photoassisted holes catalyzing each of water (H<sub>2</sub>O) and hydroxide (OH<sup>-</sup>) oxidation to form hydrogen peroxide (H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>) via two <sup>•</sup>OH. Information about material structure as well as catalyst structure at the catalytic sites as well as the atomic level in situ (at time=0 and during the course of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> catalytic production reactions) is provided for the first time and the samples at catalytic active sites at the atomic level have been systematically characterized at multiple length scales utilizing techniques ranging from quantum chemistry calculations through electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR), X-ray absorption near edge structure (XANES) spectroscopy, extended X-ray absorption fine structure (EXAFS), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and with magic-angle spinning solid-state nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy. In addition to the respective strengths provided by individual methodologies, multiscale molecular modeling has also been conducted to elucidate sample structural/compositional information coupled with an understanding of catalytic reaction process dynamics such as catalyst reusability associated with structure and properties in the catalytic system derived from seawater provided by the hybrid model which effectively integrated data derived from both classical reactive molecular dynamics simulations and associated quantum chemistry calculations.
